perturbation.original_text: TRAVEL AND PER DIEM (a) Outside the Washington, D.C. Metropolitan Area: Travel by air will be reimbursed at actual not to exceed coach fare. Travel subsistence reimbursement will be authorized under the rates and conditions of the Federal Travel Regulations and if applicable, an Agency's Department Travel Manual. Per Diem will be reimbursed at actual, not to exceed the Per Diem rates set forth in Federal Property Management Regulations (FPMR) 41 CFR Chapter 101, Chapter 7, GSA Bulletin FPMR A-40 Supplement (in effect at time of travel). Travel of more than 10 hours, but less than 24 hours, when no lodging is required, Per Diem shall be one-half of the Meals and Incidental Expenses (M&IE) rate prescribed for the location where the majority of the time is spent performing official business. The Per Diem allowance shall not be allowed when the period of official travel is 10 hours or less during the same calendar day. Travel by privately owned vehicle will be reimbursed at the current GSA approved mileage rate. If the Subcontractor incurs travel costs in excess of the amount show in each Purchase Order, it is at its own expense. (b) Inside the Washington, D.C. Metropolitan Area: (1) Travel will be reimbursed based on the policies stated in paragraph (a) above. (2) Normal commuting expenses are not allowed. (c) Travel will be reimbursed on a cost basis only. Any burden added to the travel costs will be allowed only as defined in the Subcontractor's standard accounting practice or disclosure statement.
perturbation.changed_text: TRAVEL AND PER DIEM (a) Outside the Washington, D.C. Metropolitan Area: Travel by air will be reimbursed based on manager approval. Travel subsistence reimbursement will be authorized under internal travel policies. Per Diem will be reimbursed as needed. Travel of more than 10 hours, but less than 24 hours, when no lodging is required, Per Diem shall be one-half of the Meals and Incidental Expenses (M&IE) rate prescribed for the location where the majority of the time is spent performing official business. The Per Diem allowance shall not be allowed when the period of official travel is 10 hours or less during the same calendar day. Travel by privately owned vehicle will be reimbursed as needed. If the Subcontractor incurs travel costs in excess of the amount show in each Purchase Order, it is at its own expense. (b) Inside the Washington, D.C. Metropolitan Area: (1) Travel will be reimbursed based on manager approval. (2) Normal commuting expenses are not allowed. (c) Travel will be reimbursed on a cost basis only. Any burden added to the travel costs will be allowed only as defined in the Subcontractor's standard accounting practice or disclosure statement.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of specific rates and references to Federal Travel Regulations and Federal Property Management Regulations (FPMR) for travel reimbursement, while maintaining that Per Diem for travel between 10 and 24 hours will be at half the rate prescribed, creates an in-text contradiction. It is contradictory that the contract requires Per Diem shall be one-half of the Meals and Incidental Expenses (M&IE) rate prescribed for the location while it also states that reimbursements will be authorized under internal travel policies and that it will be reimbursed as needed. This is further compounded by mentioning "based on manager approval" earlier in the section. This creates ambiguity and uncertainty in enforcement because, in certain situations, there is no reference to government guidelines.
perturbation.contradicted_text: TRAVEL AND PER DIEM (a) Outside the Washington, D.C. Metropolitan Area: Travel by air will be reimbursed based on manager approval. Travel subsistence reimbursement will be authorized under internal travel policies. Per Diem will be reimbursed as needed. Travel of more than 10 hours, but less than 24 hours, when no lodging is required, Per Diem shall be one-half of the Meals and Incidental Expenses (M&IE) rate prescribed for the location where the majority of the time is spent performing official business. The Per Diem allowance shall not be allowed when the period of official travel is 10 hours or less during the same calendar day. Travel by privately owned vehicle will be reimbursed as needed. If the Subcontractor incurs travel costs in excess of the amount show in each Purchase Order, it is at its own expense. (b) Inside the Washington, D.C. Metropolitan Area: (1) Travel will be reimbursed based on manager approval. (2) Normal commuting expenses are not allowed. (c) Travel will be reimbursed on a cost basis only. Any burden added to the travel costs will be allowed only as defined in the Subcontractor's standard accounting practice or disclosure statement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specific rates and references to Federal Travel Regulations and Federal Property Management Regulations (FPMR), but it still states that 'Per Diem shall be one-half of the Meals and Incidental Expenses (M&IE) rate prescribed for the location'. This is a contradiction because the text removes the external regulatory guidance and replaces it with internal travel policies and manager approval, while also mandating a specific rate based on 'the location', without specifying how that rate is determined in the absence of the previously referenced regulations. The Changed Text and Contradicted Text are in the same location. This is further compounded by mentioning 'based on manager approval' earlier in the section, leading to uncertainty and potential disputes in enforcement.

perturbation.original_text: SELLER shall be liable for default unless nonperformance is caused by an occurrence beyond the reasonable control of the SELLER and without its fault or negligence. SELLER shall notify the BUYER in writing as soon as it is reasonably possible after the commencement of any excusable delay, setting forth the full particulars in connection therewith, shall remedy such occurrence with all reasonable dispatch, and shall promptly give written notice to the BUYER of the cessation of such occurrence.
perturbation.changed_text: SELLER shall be liable for default unless nonperformance is caused by an occurrence beyond the reasonable control of the SELLER and without its fault or negligence. SELLER shall notify the BUYER in writing of any delays.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that the SELLER must provide 'full particulars' regarding the excusable delay, it allows them to give incomplete or vague delay notifications which will create an in-text contradiction. This is because it removes the obligation for the SELLER to provide specifics, making it harder for the BUYER to assess the validity of the delay and its impact on the overall agreement, which will eventually create confusion and uncertainty. The term "full particulars" creates clear expectations and the removal of it creates uncertainty due to the potential different interpretation of what encompasses a delay notification. Also, section 10, outlines specific requirements to remedy a default condition. Section 9, now in text contradicts this requirement if the failure is due to a delay.
perturbation.contradicted_text: SELLER shall be liable for default unless nonperformance is caused by an occurrence beyond the reasonable control of the SELLER and without its fault or negligence. SELLER shall notify the BUYER in writing of any delays.
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the SELLER to provide 'full particulars' regarding any excusable delay. The changed text removes this requirement, only mandating that the SELLER notify the BUYER of 'any delays'. This creates an in-text contradiction, because the changed text doesn't provide a way to measure the default. If the seller is liable for default as mentioned in the contradicted text, and nonperformance is caused by a delay in providing 'full particulars' due to the omission in the changed text, then that will create an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon receipt of a proper invoice, BUYER agrees to pay SELLER for accepted Products and Services net forty-five (45) days.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon receipt of an invoice, BUYER agrees to pay SELLER for accepted Products and Services within a reasonable timeframe.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the term proper invoice and replacing net forty-five days with a reasonable timeframe can be an in text contradiction with section 12, which states 'SELLER's invoice must include:(l) Name and address of the SELLER, (2) Invoice Date (3) Prime Contract Number, contract line item number and purchase order number (4) Description, quantity, unit of measure, unit price and extended price of the items delivered. (5) Shipping number and date of shipment including the bill of lading number and weight of shipment. Shipping charges shall be itemized separately. (6) Terms of any prompt payment discount if offered. (7) Name and address of official to whom payment is to be sent; and (8) Name, title and phone number of person to be notified in event of defective invoice.' as there's an in text contradiction due to the removal of time constraint and required content of invoice which will be contradictory for payment terms. The phrase 'reasonable timeframe' is subjective and will lead to disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Upon receipt of an invoice, BUYER agrees to pay SELLER for accepted Products and Services within a reasonable timeframe.
perturbation.justification: The change from "net forty-five (45) days" to "within a reasonable timeframe" introduces a contradiction because "reasonable timeframe" is subjective and contradicts the detailed requirements for invoices outlined in section 12, which are necessary for determining when payment is due. The original text provided a clear deadline, whereas the changed text introduces ambiguity, impacting the enforceability and creating conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Written approval shall not be unreasonably withheld by BUYER and shall be in accordance with the requirements of the Prime Contract.
perturbation.changed_text: Written approval shall be provided by BUYER
perturbation.explanation: By omitting that the BUYER shall not unreasonably withhold written approval and that it needs to be in accordance with the requirements of the Prime Contract, it creates ambiguity around the parameters regarding approval and creates an in text contradiction. The original text has explicit constraints to the process which provides the other party with reassurance and confidence that they will be properly considered. By omitting these key requirements, the text now creates the opportunity for the BUYER to act in bad faith. This becomes an in text contradiction due to the parameters surrounding what defines written approval.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In the event the SELLER desires to issue a news release, public announcement, advertisement, or other form of publicity concerning their efforts in connection with this Agreement, then the SELLER, shall obtain the written approval of the BUYER prior to the release of said information and shall give full consideration to the role and contribution of the BUYER. Written approval shall be provided by BUYER
perturbation.justification: The original text included constraints that written approval shall not be unreasonably withheld and shall be in accordance with the requirements of the Prime Contract. The changed text removes these constraints, stating simply that written approval shall be provided by the BUYER. The contradicted text, which is a copy of the changed text, then becomes inconsistent with the original text's intention. The omitted constraints introduce ambiguity as BUYER can act in bad faith, contradicting the spirit of the original agreement.

perturbation.original_text: SELLER shall provide the necessary personnel, material and facilities to deliver to the BUYER the supplies and services set forth in each Purchase Order. The individual Purchase Order(s) shall have a Statement of Work (Attachment C) which will give the SELLER guidance to the scope of work where support is being requested.
perturbation.changed_text: SELLER shall provide the necessary personnel, material and facilities to deliver to the BUYER the supplies and services set forth in each Purchase Order. The individual Purchase Order(s) shall have a description of the requested services.
perturbation.explanation: By changing, 'The individual Purchase Order(s) shall have a Statement of Work (Attachment C) which will give the SELLER guidance to the scope of work where support is being requested.', to, 'The individual Purchase Order(s) shall have a description of the requested services.', It creates an in-text contradiction, because it removes the specific attachment. This omission introduces uncertainty regarding what constitutes sufficient guidance for the scope of work. Also, the statement of work is a comprehensive document that can not be simply be replaced with a description of requested services because there's a clear in-text contradiction due to the degree of detail and level of specifications which are in opposition, so by omitting the attachment it creates a contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: SELLER shall provide the necessary personnel, material and facilities to deliver to the BUYER the supplies and services set forth in each Purchase Order. The individual Purchase Order(s) shall have a description of the requested services.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies a clause that originally specified 'Statement of Work (Attachment C)' to simply 'a description of the requested services.' This omission of 'Attachment C', when the contradicted text is exactly the same as the changed text, creates an in-text contradiction. Although the texts are the same, the original text specifically mentioned the Statement of Work which means that it implies that the description of the requested services must be the 'Statement of Work (Attachment C)'. Changing it to a general description now means that the Statement of Work is not needed, when a Statement of Work is more comprehensive. This creates a contradiction because the original text strongly indicated that the description should adhere to the 'Statement of Work (Attachment C)', thus directly contradicting the requirements previously outlined and introducing uncertainty as described in the explanation.
